Performing initial audit of PKGBUILD to ensure that we can safely run `makepkg`
LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD defines a standard source array with a tarball from the project's GitHub release page (https://github.com/satisfactorymodding/SatisfactoryModManager) and a local .desktop file. The tarball URL is deterministic based on the tagged version `v${pkgver}`, which is safe and expected for source retrieval. The `sha512sums` array provides fixed checksums for both sources, preventing undetected tampering during download. No command substitution, dynamic URL construction, or untrusted code execution is present in the global scope or `pkgver()` function (which is absent, so the pkgver is static). Therefore, running `makepkg --nobuild --nodeps --noprepare` or `makepkg --printsrcinfo` will safely fetch and verify only the declared sources without executing any untrusted or dynamic code.
</details>
<summary>
No dynamic code execution during source fetch
</summary>
</security_assessment>

+ Initial PKGBUILD audit complete [grey50]-- No dynamic code execution during source fetch[/]

LLM auditresponse for satisfactory-mod-manager.desktop:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The file `satisfactory-mod-manager.desktop` is a standard desktop entry file used to integrate the application into the desktop environment. It specifies the application name, executable command (`satisfactory-mod-manager %u`), icon, and MIME type handling for the `smmanager` scheme. The `Exec` field calls the application with a URL argument, which is normal behavior for handling custom URI schemes in games and mod tools.

All fields conform to the freedesktop.org desktop entry specification. The `MimeType`, `Categories`, and `StartupWMClass` entries are appropriate for a game-related mod manager. There are no shell commands, script invocations, or embedded code. The file does not perform any file operations, network requests, or system modifications directly. It is a static configuration file used solely for application launching and desktop integration.

While the MIME type `x-scheme-handler/smmanager` allows the application to handle custom URLs, this functionality is expected for mod managers that install mods via URI links. The risk of such handlers is mitigated by user confirmation dialogs in most desktop environments. There is no indication of malicious intent or behavior in this file.
</details>
<summary>
Standard desktop entry; no malicious content
</summary>
</security_assessment>

[1/2] Reviewing PKGBUILD...
+ Reviewed satisfactory-mod-manager.desktop. Status: SAFE -- Standard desktop entry; no malicious content
LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD is for the Satisfactory Mod Manager, a tool to manage mods for the game Satisfactory. It retrieves the source code from the official GitHub repository using a tagged release (v3.1.0), which is a standard and expected practice. The `sha512sums` are provided and match the archive, ensuring integrity. No checksums are marked as 'SKIP', and no VCS sources (like git+) are used—only a static tarball, so reproducibility is preserved.

The build process runs `go-task build`, which is consistent with the upstream project's use of Task (a Go-based task runner) for builds. There are no network requests, `curl`/`wget` calls, or `eval` statements in the script. The `package()` function installs the binary, desktop file, and icon assets using standard `install` commands into appropriate system directories. All dependencies listed (`yarn`, `nodejs`, `wails`, `pnpm`, etc.) are expected for building a frontend/backend application and appear legitimate in context.

There is no obfuscated code, suspicious command execution, or unexpected file manipulation. The operations are confined to the build directory and destination package directory. The maintainer provides a desktop file and installs icons via standard FHS-compliant paths. No data exfiltration or system modification beyond packaging scope is present. The package adheres to standard AUR packaging practices and poses no signs of supply-chain compromise.
</details>
<summary>
Standard build, no malicious behavior detected</summary>
</security_assessment>

LLM auditresponse for window.go:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The provided Go file `window.go` is part of a desktop application backend using the Wails framework. It handles window state management such as position, size, maximization, and visibility across displays. The code periodically checks the window's position and size via `wailsRuntime` and ensures the window remains visible by centering it on the primary display if it's off-screen. It also saves window state to persistent settings when changes occur.

All operations are confined to local window state management and use standard Wails runtime APIs. The file interacts with system displays via `utils.GetDisplayBounds()` and logs warnings if no displays are found or if display dimensions are invalid. Settings are saved through `settings.SaveSettings()`, which is a controlled operation for user preference persistence. There are no network requests, file system manipulations beyond settings, or use of dangerous constructs like `eval`, `os/exec`, or obfuscated calls.

No suspicious or malicious behavior is present. The code aligns with expected behavior for a desktop GUI application managing window geometry and user preferences. All dependencies are from the project's own backend modules or the Wails framework. There is no data exfiltration, unauthorized execution, or supply-chain risk.
</details>
<summary>
Safe: Normal window management with no malicious behavior
</summary>
</security_assessment>

LLM auditresponse for app.go:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The provided Go file `app.go` is part of the `satisfactory-mod-manager` backend and defines a minimal application struct that interfaces with the Wails runtime (used for building desktop apps with Go and web frontend). The code exposes a `CloseAndRestart` method that sets a flag and calls `wailsRuntime.Quit`, a standard API provided by Wails for cleanly shutting down the application. There are no external network calls, file operations, system command executions, or data exfiltration behaviors.

The code uses standard dependencies: `github.com/wailsapp/wails/v2` for runtime control and a project-local backend package for shared context. The `common.AppContext` is assumed to be initialized elsewhere and passed in correctly. No obfuscated logic, suspicious payloads, or dangerous operations (e.g., `exec`, `eval`, `os.Open` on sensitive paths) are present. The use of `Quit` is inline with Wails application lifecycle management.

The file does not perform any packaging-specific or system-modifying tasks; it is pure application logic. All behaviors are consistent with the application’s purpose: managing lifecycle events for a desktop GUI app. There is no evidence of injected malicious code or supply-chain compromise.
</details>
<summary>
No malicious behavior; standard Wails app lifecycle code
</summary>
</security_assessment>

LLM auditresponse for interactions.go:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The file `interactions.go` contains Go code that defines various UI and system interaction functions for a desktop application built with Wails, a framework for creating desktop apps using Go and web technologies. The functions handle window sizing, file/directory dialogs, opening external resources, and revealing files in the system file manager.

All operations performed in this file are consistent with legitimate desktop application behavior. For example, `ShowInExplorer` uses OS-appropriate commands (`explorer`, `open`, or D-Bus calls) to reveal a file in the system's file manager—this is standard functionality in file management tools. The use of `exec.Command` is limited to calling well-known system executables with user-provided paths, which poses no inherent risk beyond normal application scope. Similarly, `OpenExternal` uses the `browser` package to open a file or URL, which is typical for "open with default app" features.

There are no signs of code obfuscation, unauthorized network requests, data exfiltration, or execution of downloaded content. The dependencies used (e.g., `wailsRuntime`, `dbus`, `browser`) are standard and appropriately scoped. No files are written, and no sensitive data is transmitted. The code interacts with the host system only in ways expected for a desktop application managing user files and UI events.
</details>
<summary>
No malicious behavior; standard desktop app interactions</summary>
</security_assessment>

LLM auditresponse for debug_info.go:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The provided Go code is part of a debug information generation feature in the Satisfactory Mod Manager (SMM). It collects and packages diagnostic data—such as game logs, installation metadata, active profiles, and mod versions—into a ZIP file for troubleshooting purposes.

The code reads local log files (e.g., FactoryGame.log), redacts sensitive information like friend nicknames from Steam/Epic to prevent privacy leaks, and includes metadata such as installation paths (redacted), selected profiles, and mod versions. All file operations are confined to user-accessible diagnostic data, and all writes are directed to a user-chosen location via a save dialog. The collected data is limited to application-specific logs and configuration, consistent with legitimate debugging functionality.

There are no network calls, no use of dangerous execution functions (like eval), and no attempts to exfiltrate data. The use of `archive/zip` is appropriately scoped to creating a debug bundle locally. The code includes proper error handling and logging. While installation paths are included, they are redacted using `utils.RedactPath`. This file represents standard, safe diagnostic tooling with privacy considerations.
</details>
<summary>
Safely collects and redacts app debug data
</summary>
</security_assessment>

LLM auditresponse for graphql.ts:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The provided `graphql.ts` file is a TypeScript module that configures and initializes a URQL GraphQL client for use in a Svelte application. It imports standard libraries from `@urql` and uses them to create a type-safe, cache-enabled GraphQL client pointed at a runtime-provided API endpoint. The `initializeGraphQLClient` function accepts a URL and configures exchanges for caching (using a schema-import from a local project path) and for persisted queries, which are legitimate optimizations in the URQL ecosystem.

There are no signs of malicious behavior. The code performs no network requests outside of the expected GraphQL operations via `fetchExchange`, and the endpoint is passed in as a parameter (not hardcoded to a suspicious domain). It does not execute external scripts, perform unsafe deserialization, or include obfuscated code. The use of `@urql/exchange-graphcache` with custom key resolvers and schema is consistent with proper client-side caching practices. All dependencies are pulled from official npm packages (`@urql/svelte`, `@urql/exchange-graphcache`, etc.), and the use of `fetchExchange` is standard for browser-based GraphQL clients.

The file operates purely within the application's runtime logic and contributes to the declared purpose of a mod manager—interacting with a backend API to retrieve mod and user data. No file system access, shell commands, or suspicious runtime evals are present. This is a safe, idiomatic GraphQL client setup.
</details>
<summary>
Safe GraphQL client configuration with no malicious code
</summary>
</security_assessment>

LLM auditresponse for darwin_app.go:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The provided Go file `darwin_app.go` is part of the Satisfactory Mod Manager backend and implements update logic for macOS (Darwin) applications. It uses the `github.com/minio/selfupdate` library to securely apply binary updates by verifying checksums and handling file replacement atomically. The code downloads an update via a reader, checks it using `selfupdate.PrepareAndCheckBinary`, extracts a zip archive containing a macOS `.app` bundle to a temporary location, and then commits the update safely using `selfupdate.CommitBinary`. The use of temporary directories, atomic file operations, and checksum validation aligns with secure update practices.

Restart functionality uses `os.StartProcess` to re-launch the application after an update, which is a common pattern in updater implementations. All paths are derived locally from the running executable, and there is no use of `eval`, `exec` with untrusted strings, or remote code execution. The file operates only on the application's own binary directory and does not access sensitive system locations, exfiltrate data, or execute external scripts. The dependencies and operations are consistent with the stated purpose: applying self-updates for a desktop application.

No obfuscated code, suspicious network calls, or dangerous system manipulations are present. The use of `selfupdate` from MinIO is reputable and commonly used for secure self-updating binaries. The extraction and renaming logic is explicit and transparent, and all file operations are scoped to the application's own directory.
</details>
<summary>
Secure self-update logic for macOS app; no malicious behavior detected</summary>
</security_assessment>

LLM auditresponse for nsis.go:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The provided Go code implements an NSIS installer updater using the `selfupdate` library. It defines a `Download` method that securely receives an io.Reader and checksum, then uses `selfupdate.PrepareAndCheckBinary` to write and verify the binary at a configured path. This is a legitimate use of self-update logic, common in update-aware applications, and does not involve unsafe sources or execution of downloaded code within the same process.

The `Apply` method constructs command-line arguments for a silent install (`/S`) of an NSIS installer, optionally scoped to all users or current user, and optionally forcing a restart. It executes the pre-downloaded installer via `exec.Command` and properly releases the process. This behavior is consistent with standard application auto-update patterns and does not exhibit signs of obfuscation, unauthorized network access, or data exfiltration.

The code operates within expected boundaries: it downloads a binary to a specified path with checksum validation, then executes it as a separate process with standard installer flags. No use of dangerous constructs like `eval`, `curl|bash`, or base64 decoding is present. The actions are transparent and aligned with the stated purpose of applying an NSIS-based update.
</details>
<summary>
Standard self-update implementation, no malicious behavior</summary>
</security_assessment>

LLM auditresponse for appimage.go:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The provided Go code implements an AppImage update mechanism using the `selfupdate` library from MinIO, which is a legitimate and well-maintained project for self-updating binaries. The code reads the `APPIMAGE` environment variable to locate the current AppImage binary and uses `selfupdate.PrepareAndCheckBinary` to verify the checksum before writing the update via `selfupdate.CommitBinary`. This is standard AppImage update behavior and does not involve unsafe operations.

The code does not perform any network operations directly; downloading must be handled externally. It only applies updates from a trusted source provided via the `file` reader, which is expected in this context. Restarting the application after an update is a common UX pattern and is implemented via a helper function (`utils.Restart()`), which is unlikely to be malicious given the context.

There are no signs of code injection, unauthorized data exfiltration, or execution of remote code. The use of `selfupdate` is appropriate for self-contained applications like AppImages. No suspicious file operations, obfuscation, or unexpected system calls are present.
</details>

LLM auditresponse for appimage.sh:
<security_assessment>
<decision>UNSAFE</decision>
<details>
The script downloads and executes multiple binaries and scripts from remote sources without any integrity verification, which constitutes a supply-chain risk. Specifically, it uses `wget` to fetch `linuxdeploy-plugin-gtk.sh`, `linuxdeploy-plugin-gstreamer.sh`, and `linuxdeploy.AppImage` from GitHub repositories over HTTPS, then immediately makes them executable and runs them. The same applies to the `AppRun` binary downloaded from AppImageKit's releases. These downloaded components are executed in an automated manner to assemble the AppDir, significantly increasing the attack surface.

While the domains (github.com, raw.githubusercontent.com) are legitimate and the projects appear related to the tooling (tauri-apps, AppImage), the lack of checksum validation or signature checks means an attacker who compromises any of these upstream projects or performs a MITM attack could inject malicious code into the build process. Furthermore, the script copies WebKit binaries from the host system (`/usr/lib*`) — a normal action — but does so unsafely via unquoted `find` and `cp --parents`, though this is less severe than the remote execution.

The core issue is the blind trust in remote content: downloading and running executables and shell scripts without verification violates secure packaging principles and enables remote code execution if any upstream source is compromised.

**Problematic snippet:**
```
wget https://raw.githubusercontent.com/tauri-apps/linuxdeploy-plugin-gtk/master/linuxdeploy-plugin-gtk.sh
chmod +x linuxdeploy-plugin-gtk.sh
```
...and similar lines execute unverified remote scripts.
</details>
<summary>
Downloads and runs unsigned remote scripts and binaries</summary>
</security_assessment>

Furthermore, the script copies WebKit binaries from the host system (`/usr/lib*`) — a normal action — but does so unsafely via unquoted `find` and `cp --parents`, though this is less severe than the remote execution.
